text: This video demonstrates robotic transabdominal intersphincteric resection with lateral pelvic lymph node dissection for patients with distal rectal cancer. A seventy-five-year-old woman had bloody stool for a year. Colonoscopy revealed rectal cancer three centimeters above the anal verge. Pelvic MRI disclosed advanced distal rectal cancer with lateral pelvic lymph node involvement.

This video demonstrates robotic transabdominal intersphincteric resection with lateral pelvic lymph node dissection for patients with distal rectal cancer. A seventy-five-year-old woman had bloody stool for a year. Colonoscopy revealed rectal cancer three centimeters above the anal verge. Pelvic MRI disclosed advanced distal rectal cancer with lateral pelvic lymph node involvement.

The surgeon performs robotic transeptominal intersphincteric resection, starting with a total mesorectal excision (TME) approach. Dissection progresses toward the pelvic floor along the vascular plane between the mesorectum and presacral fascia. After completing lateral and dorsal dissections, the anterior plane is dissected, dividing the peritoneal reflection. The dissection continues through the rectovaginal septum, mobilizing the vaginal wall with monopolar cautery to separate it from the rectum. The mesorectum is dissected up to one or two centimeters above the anorectal angle.

The surgeon performs robotic transabdominal intersphincteric resection, starting with a total mesorectal excision (TME) approach. Dissection proceeds toward the pelvic floor along the vascular plane between the mesorectum and presacral fascia. After completing lateral and dorsal dissections, the anterior plane is dissected, dividing the peritoneal reflection. The dissection continues through the rectovaginal septum, mobilizing the vaginal wall with monopolar cautery to separate it from the rectum. The mesorectum is dissected up to one or two centimeters above the anorectal angle.

The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision (TME) down to the anorectal junction and levator ani plane. The intersphincteric resection begins from the left side of the rectum, dissecting between the rectal wall and puborectal muscle, which is located medially to the pubococcygeal muscle. The dissection continues distally and circumferentially, removing partial or total internal anal sphincteric muscles while preserving external sphincteric muscles. The intersphincteric space is dissected circumferentially, and the rectum is divided using an endogia stapler.

The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision (TME) down to the anorectal junction and levator ani plane. The intersphincteric resection begins from the left side of the rectum, dissecting between the rectal wall and puborectal muscle, which is located medially to the pubococcygeal muscle. The intersphincteric dissection proceeds distally and circumferentially, removing partial or total internal anal sphincteric muscles while preserving external sphincteric muscles. The dissection of the intersphincteric space is completed circumferentially, followed by division of the rectum using an endogia stapler.

The surgeon performs pelvic lymph node dissection, resecting lymph nodes and fatty tissues in the common iliac vessel area. The left common iliac vein is visible, located in front of the S-I and L-V vertebral bodies. An enlarged lymph node is observed in the precycal area. The presacral lymph nodes overlying the sacral promontory are also removed, with careful dissection to avoid injury to the iliac vein.

The surgeon performs pelvic lymph node dissection, resecting lymph nodes and fatty tissues in the common iliac vessel area. The left common iliac vein is visible, located in front of the S-I and L-V vertebral bodies. An enlarged lymph node is observed in the precycal area. The presacral lymph nodes overlying the sacral promontory are also removed, with careful dissection to avoid injuring the iliac vein.

The surgeon dissects the lateral lymph nodes around the external iliac artery, internal iliac artery, and obturator fossa. The left ureter is identified crossing in front of the bifurcation of the common iliac artery and is carefully mobilized to prevent injury.

The surgeon dissects the lateral lymph nodes around the external iliac artery, internal iliac artery, and obturator fossa. The left ureter is visible crossing in front of the bifurcation of the common iliac artery, and it is carefully identified and mobilized to prevent injury.

The pelvic MRI shows lymph node involvement around the left internal iliac region, with visibly enlarged lymph nodes. The surgeon performs pelvic lymph node dissection, removing lymph nodes and fatty tissue within the obturator fossa and below the obturator nerve. The obturator nerve is identified medial to the external iliac artery and lateral to the superior vesicle artery. The left external iliac vein is observed traveling on the medial side of the left iliac artery.

The pelvic MRI shows lymph node involvement around the left internal iliac region, with visibly enlarged lymph nodes. The surgeon performs pelvic lymph node dissection, removing lymph nodes and fatty tissue within the obturator fossa, below the obturator nerve. The obturator nerve is identified medial to the external iliac artery and lateral to the superior vesicle artery.
The left external iliac vein is visible traveling medially to the left iliac artery. The surgeon resects the obturator lymph nodes while preserving the obturator nerve.

The surgeon resects the obturator lymph nodes while preserving the obturator nerve. Under laparoscopic vision, a stable colon anal anastomosis is performed, with the post-operative endoscope confirming the anastomosis line is positioned just around the dentate line.

The surgeon performs a stable colon anal anastomosis under laparoscopic vision. The immediate postoperative endoscopic view confirms the anastomosis line is located just around the dentate line. The gross pathology reveals an adequate distal safety margin and circumferential resection margin.

The gross pathology shows an adequate distal safety margin and a circumferential resection margin. The operation time was five hours and seventeen minutes. The length of post-operative hospital stay was twelve days, and no surgical complication occurred.

The robotic approach can be safely and efficiently applied for the surgery of patients with advanced distal rectal cancer requiring transabdominal intersphincteric resection and lateral pelvic lymph node dissection.
